posts.title: AI agents replaced my deploy pipeline
posts.hook: I automated my entire deploy pipeline with Claude Code last week. Took 45 minutes. The pipeline I spent 3 months building manually.
posts.body:
I automated my entire deploy pipeline with Claude Code last week. Took 45 minutes. The pipeline I spent 3 months building manually.

Here's what actually happened. I pointed an agent at my CI/CD config, told it "make this work for preview deploys on every PR," and walked away to make coffee.

Came back to a working pipeline. Tests running. Preview URLs spinning up. Slack notifications wired.

The part that got me... it found a race condition in my webhook handler that I'd been ignoring for weeks. Fixed it without being asked.

This isn't "AI will take your job" energy. This is "AI will take the mass of your job you secretly hate doing." The config files. The YAML debugging. The "why is this env var not loading" at 11pm.

What's left is the interesting stuff. Architecture decisions. Figuring out what to build next. Talking to users.

I'm mass more productive now, but not because I type faster. Because I stopped spending 60% of my week on plumbing.

The developers who thrive in 2 years won't be the ones who write the most code. They'll be the ones who orchestrate agents to write code while they focus on problems worth solving.
posts.cta: What's the most tedious part of your workflow that an agent could eat today?

remixes.label: time-arbitrage
remixes.hook: The ROI on AI agents isn't productivity. It's time arbitrage.
remixes.body:
The ROI on AI agents isn't productivity. It's time arbitrage.

I used to spend Sunday nights debugging YAML configs for CI/CD. Fixing flaky tests. Wiring up Slack webhooks that broke every time someone renamed a channel.

Last week I described what I wanted in plain English and let Claude Code figure out the implementation. Preview deploys on every PR. Automated test gates. Notifications to the right channel with the right context.

The agent didn't just save me time on the task. It eliminated an entire category of work I was doing every week. That's the part people miss when they talk about AI productivity gains. It's not about doing the same work faster. It's about deleting work from your schedule permanently.

The pipeline it built is cleaner than what I had before. Not because AI is smarter than me. Because it doesn't carry the baggage of "well, we've always done it this way" that accumulates in every codebase.

I've started auditing my week for these patterns. Tasks I repeat. Tasks that follow predictable rules. Tasks where the hard part is remembering the right syntax, not making judgment calls. Every one of those is a candidate for deletion.
remixes.cta: What recurring task on your calendar could an agent just... remove entirely?

remixes.label: before-after-breakdown
remixes.hook: Three months of DevOps work. 45 minutes of prompting. Same result. Here's the honest breakdown.
remixes.body:
Three months of DevOps work. 45 minutes of prompting. Same result. Here's the honest breakdown.

When I built my deploy pipeline by hand, here's where the time went. Two weeks learning the CI provider's quirks. A week on Docker caching strategies that saved 90 seconds per build. Three weeks debugging environment variable propagation across staging and production. The rest was edge cases, retries, and "why is this flaky on Tuesdays."

When the agent built it, it skipped the exploration phase entirely. It already knew the patterns. It didn't need to learn through trial and error. It just applied the standard approach and moved on.

: most of my three months wasn't solving hard problems. It was navigating documentation, remembering syntax, and handling config drift. That's exactly the work agents are best at.

The parts that actually required judgment, like deciding which tests should block deploys vs. warn, what the notification strategy should be, how to handle rollbacks. Those still needed me. The agent asked. I answered. It implemented.

This is the real split happening in engineering right now. Judgment work stays human. Implementation work gets automated. The question is how much of your week is actually judgment.
remixes.cta: Be honest. What percentage of your engineering work is judgment vs. implementation?

tones.advisor:
If you're still manually maintaining CI/CD pipelines, I'd recommend stepping back and evaluating which parts actually require human judgment. In my experience, the vast majority of pipeline work, test configuration, preview deploys, notification wiring, follows well-established patterns that agents handle reliably.

The approach I recommend: start with your most tedious, most-repeated pipeline task. Describe the desired end state to an agent in plain language. Let it generate the config. Review what it produces.

You'll find that 80% of the output is correct on the first pass. The remaining 20% is where your expertise matters, edge cases, rollback strategies, environment-specific behavior. That's the work worth your time.

The teams I see moving fastest aren't replacing engineers with agents. They're shifting engineers from implementation to architecture and review. The deploy pipeline is just the most obvious starting point because it's high-effort, low-judgment, and immediately measurable.

My recommendation: audit your next sprint for tasks where the hard part is syntax and configuration, not decision-making. Those are your first candidates.
tones.provocateur:
You spent three months on a deploy pipeline and you're proud of it? That's not craftsmanship. That's Stockholm syndrome with YAML files.

We've been telling ourselves that DevOps is skilled work that requires deep expertise. And some of it is. But be honest about how much of your pipeline work is actually copy-pasting from Stack Overflow and tweaking environment variables until the build goes green.

An agent did it in 45 minutes because the "hard part" was never hard. It was tedious. We confused the two because tedious work that requires focus feels important while you're doing it.

The engineers who are going to thrive aren't the ones who can write the most complex GitHub Actions workflow from memory. They're the ones who can describe what they want clearly enough for an agent to build it, then review the output with enough context to catch what matters.

If your main value-add is remembering CI/CD syntax, you should be worried. If your main value-add is knowing what should be tested, what should block deploys, and how failures should propagate, you're fine.

Most people don't want to hear which category they're actually in.

posts.title: GTM engineering is just SDR work with APIs
posts.hook: Every GTM engineer I respect started in the trenches. Not in strategy decks. In Salesforce, building buying committees by hand.
posts.body:
Every GTM engineer I respect started in the trenches. Not in strategy decks. In Salesforce, building buying committees by hand.

The pattern I keep seeing: companies hire revops people to "build the GTM engine" and get dashboards. They hire former SDRs who learned to code and get pipelines that actually generate revenue.

The difference is knowing what matters at the contact level. Which signals actually convert. What a real buying committee looks like vs. what a CRM schema says one looks like.

Last month I built an enrichment pipeline for a client. Apollo for contacts, Clay for enrichment, Instantly for delivery. Standard stack. But the scoring model that made it work came from years of manually qualifying leads. Knowing that a Director of RevOps at a 200-person SaaS company who just posted about switching CRMs is a better lead than a VP who hasn't posted in 6 months.

That intuition doesn't come from a playbook. It comes from sending thousands of emails and watching what actually gets replies.

Tools are commoditized. Everyone has access to the same APIs. The edge is knowing which data points to weight and why. That's practitioner knowledge, not engineering knowledge.
posts.cta: If you're building GTM systems, what's the one signal you weight highest that most people ignore?

inspired_by.summary: Discusses building a GTM engineering function from scratch, integrating outbound automation tools like Apollo and Instantly to achieve 10x pipeline growth. Shares a framework for sales ops teams to align with revenue operations for scalable lead gen. Emphasizes data-driven content strategy to boost reply rates by 40%.

remixes.label: signal-vs-dashboard
remixes.hook: Your revenue dashboard is lying to you. Not because the data is wrong. Because you're measuring the wrong things.
remixes.body:
Your revenue dashboard is lying to you. Not because the data is wrong. Because you're measuring the wrong things.

I've seen the same pattern at a dozen companies. They hire a revops person. That person builds beautiful dashboards. Pipeline velocity. Win rates by segment. Activity metrics per rep.

Six months later, revenue is flat and everyone is confused because the dashboards all look green.

The problem: dashboards measure what happened. GTM engineering builds systems that make things happen. Different skill set entirely.

The best GTM engineers I know came up through SDR work. They sat in the chair. They felt the difference between a warm signal and noise. They know that a VP of Engineering downloading a whitepaper means nothing, but that same VP joining a Slack community about your problem space means everything.

That intuition, knowing which signals predict pipeline and which ones just look good in a report, is what separates a GTM engineer from a revops analyst. You can't learn it from a dashboard. You learn it from doing the outreach yourself and watching what converts.

The companies winning at outbound right now have someone who can both read the signals and build the pipes. Not one or the other.
remixes.cta: What's a signal you started tracking that actually moved your pipeline numbers?

remixes.label: career-path-flip
remixes.hook: The fastest path to a six-figure GTM engineering role in 2026? Start as an SDR. Seriously.
remixes.body:
The fastest path to a six-figure GTM engineering role in 2026? Start as an SDR. Seriously.

The job market is flooded with people who can build Salesforce workflows, write Python scripts, and set up Zapier automations. What's scarce is people who can do all that AND know which contact signals actually matter.

I've watched two types of people try to break into GTM engineering. Type one learned revops tools first. They build clean systems that produce clean data that nobody acts on. Type two did SDR work, got frustrated with bad tooling, and learned to code to fix their own problems.

Type two ships revenue. Every time.

Because they've sat through the pain of a badly enriched lead list. They've experienced the difference between a "personalized" email that's obviously templated and one that actually gets a reply. They know what a real buying signal looks like because they've chased hundreds of fake ones.

You can teach an SDR to write Python. You can't teach a Python developer the gut instinct for which accounts are actually in-market. That pattern recognition comes from reps, not from tutorials.

If you're an SDR who's been automating parts of your own workflow, you're closer to a GTM engineering career than most CS grads.
remixes.cta: SDRs who learned to code: what was the first thing you automated? That's usually the best indicator of GTM engineering potential.

The pattern I consistently see in high-performing GTM teams is this: the people building the systems have direct outbound experience. Not theoretical knowledge from a course. Actual time in the seat making calls and sending cold emails.

Here's why that matters. When you've done the outreach yourself, you understand signal quality at a visceral level. You know that a job posting for your buyer persona's role means budget exists. You know that a company switching from a competitor's tool shows up in technographic data weeks before it shows up in intent platforms.

My recommendation for anyone building GTM infrastructure: spend at least 3 months doing the outreach manually before automating anything. Map every signal that led to a real conversation. Then build your pipeline around those specific signals, not the ones your data vendor tells you matter.

The companies I've seen get the best results treat GTM engineering as a progression from SDR work, not a parallel track. The systems thinker who has never felt the pain of a bad lead list builds systems that look good on paper but don't generate pipeline.

Invest in people who've done the work. Then give them the tools to scale what they already know works.

RevOps is a cost center pretending to be a growth function. There, I said it.

Most revops teams produce dashboards, not revenue. They'll spend six weeks building a lead scoring model based on firmographic data that has zero correlation with actual close rates. Then they'll present it in a deck with confidence intervals.

Meanwhile, the former SDR who taught herself Python is pulling technographic data, cross-referencing it with job postings, and building targeted lists that actually convert. No deck. No scoring model. Just pipeline.

The difference is visceral. One person knows what a real buying signal feels like because they've been ignored by 10,000 prospects. The other person read about buying signals in a blog post and built a Salesforce workflow around it.

Stop hiring revops people to build your GTM engine. Hire SDRs who got tired of bad tools and learned to build their own. They'll ship more pipeline in a month than your revops team ships in a quarter.

Controversial? Maybe. But go check the pipeline attribution numbers. The answer is usually sitting right there.

posts.title: Memory is the moat for AI agents
posts.hook: The AI agent discourse is all about tool calling. Nobody's talking about memory. That's the actual hard problem.
posts.body:
The AI agent discourse is all about tool calling. Nobody's talking about memory. That's the actual hard problem.

I run a personal agent system that writes daily blog posts, manages my CRM, drafts outreach, and tracks my content pipeline. It works because it remembers things across sessions.

Not "vector search over old conversations" memory. Structured memory. It knows my voice rules. It knows which clients I'm working with. It knows what I shipped yesterday and what's blocked today.

Without that, every session starts from zero. The agent is smart but amnesiac. Useful for one-shots, useless for workflows.

Karpathy's right that the architecture of next-gen agents needs memory systems front and center. But here's what I've learned building this in production: the hard part isn't storing memories. It's knowing which memories matter right now.

My system has a memory index that gets loaded on every session start. First 200 lines. Curated. Categorized. Ruthlessly pruned. Because an agent with too much memory is just as broken as one with none. It drowns in context.

The teams building agents that actually work in production will be the ones who nail memory management. Not the ones with the fanciest tool integrations.
posts.cta: How are you handling memory in your agent workflows? Genuine question, I'm still iterating on this daily.

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Explores the architecture of next-gen AI agents for autonomous task execution, focusing on memory systems and tool-calling APIs. Critiques current developer tools lacking multi-agent orchestration. Predicts AI infrastructure shifts towards decentralized compute in the coming months.

remixes.label: architecture-deep-dive
remixes.hook: I've been running a personal agent system for months. The tool calling works fine. The memory architecture nearly killed the project three times.
remixes.body:
I've been running a personal agent system for months. The tool calling works fine. The memory architecture nearly killed the project three times.

Here's what I learned the hard way. Vector search over past conversations sounds elegant. In practice, it retrieves tangentially related context that confuses the agent more than it helps. "Remember that time we discussed databases" pulls up twelve conversations and the agent picks the wrong one.

What actually works: structured memory with explicit categories. My agent has a file-based memory system with frontmatter metadata. User preferences in one place. Project context in another. Feedback and corrections in a third. Each memory has a description field so the system can judge relevance without reading the full content.

The breakthrough was separating what to remember from how to retrieve it. Most agent memory systems mash these together. They store everything as embeddings and hope retrieval handles the rest. It doesn't.

My system stores memories as markdown files with typed categories: user, feedback, project, reference. An index file maps them all. Retrieval is just reading the index and pulling relevant files. No embeddings. No vector database. Just files and metadata.

Is it sophisticated? No. Does it work reliably at my scale? Every single day.

The agents that stick around won't be the ones with the most tools. They'll be the ones that actually know you after six months of use.
remixes.cta: What's your memory architecture look like? Structured files, vector DB, something else? I'm genuinely curious what's working for others.

remixes.label: product-critique
remixes.hook: Every AI agent product I've tried forgets who I am between sessions. This is the biggest unsolved problem in the space and barely anyone is working on it.
remixes.body:
Every AI agent product I've tried forgets who I am between sessions. This is the biggest unsolved problem in the space and barely anyone is working on it.

Think about your best human collaborator. What makes them effective? They remember your preferences. They know your past decisions. They don't ask you the same question twice. They build on previous context.

Now think about your AI agent. Every session starts from zero. You re-explain your stack. You re-state your preferences. You correct the same mistakes you corrected yesterday. It's like working with someone who has amnesia.

The industry is obsessed with tool calling. Can it browse the web? Can it write code? Can it call APIs? These are solved problems. The hard problem is continuity.

I built my own solution because nothing on the market handles this well. Persistent memory files that track who I am, what I've told the agent not to do, what projects are active, and where to find information in external systems. The agent reads these at session start and writes new memories as it learns.

The difference is night and day. My agent knows my voice rules. It knows my tech stack. It knows I hate em dashes. It never asks me these things anymore.

Whichever company solves persistent memory at scale wins the agent market. Not the company with the most tool integrations.
remixes.cta: Is memory the reason you keep going back to your most-used AI tool? Or is it something else?

Karpathy's right that the architecture of next-gen agents needs memory systems front and center. But here's what I've learned building this in production: the hard part isn't storing memories. It's knowing which memories matter right now.

tones.advisor:
If you're building agent workflows, I'd strongly recommend investing in memory architecture before adding more tools. The pattern I've seen fail repeatedly: teams add fifteen tool integrations, ship the agent, and then wonder why users drop off after the first week.

Users drop off because the agent doesn't remember them. Every session feels like a first session.

Here's the approach I recommend. Start with four memory categories: user context (who they are and how they work), feedback (corrections they've given you), project state (what's active and why), and references (where to find information in external systems). Store these as structured files with metadata, not as embeddings.

Vector search has its place, but for agent memory it introduces retrieval uncertainty you don't want. When your agent needs to know the user's voice preferences, you want deterministic lookup, not "most similar embedding."

The index should be lightweight. A single file that maps memory names to descriptions. The agent reads the index at session start, pulls relevant memories, and writes new ones as it learns. No database required at small scale.

Prioritize feedback memories highest. These are the corrections that prevent your agent from making the same mistake twice. Without them, every conversation repeats the same calibration period.

Memory is the moat. Tools are commoditized. Memory is what makes an agent feel like your agent.
tones.provocateur:
The AI agent industry is building race cars and forgetting to install steering wheels.

Every demo is the same. Look, our agent can call 50 APIs. Look, it can browse the web. Look, it can write code and run tests. Cool. Does it remember my name tomorrow?

No. No it does not.

We're spending billions on tool-calling infrastructure and treating memory as an afterthought. Slap a vector database on conversation logs, call it "memory," ship it. Users hate it but the demo looks great.

Real memory isn't retrieval-augmented generation over your chat history. It's structured knowledge that accumulates over time. My preferences. My corrections. My project context. Stored explicitly, recalled deterministically. Not "here are the 5 most similar past conversations" which is wrong half the time.

I built my own memory system because the market offerings are embarrassing. Markdown files with metadata. An index file. No embeddings, no vector DB, no fancy infrastructure. It works better than every "AI memory" product I've tried.

The company that solves persistent, structured agent memory wins this entire market. But nobody's working on it because it's not as demo-friendly as showing an agent book a flight.

posts.title: Outbound personalization is broken
posts.hook: "Personalization at scale" is the most abused phrase in sales ops right now. Most of what people call personalized is just merge fields with extra steps.
posts.body:
"Personalization at scale" is the most abused phrase in sales ops right now. Most of what people call personalized is just merge fields with extra steps.

"Hey {first_name}, I saw {company} just {trigger_event}..." is not personalization. It's a template with variables. The recipient knows it. Their spam filter knows it.

Real personalization means your email couldn't have been sent to anyone else. It references something specific they said, built, or published. It connects their problem to your solution in a way that requires actually understanding both.

That's hard to automate. But not impossible.

The pipeline I've been running uses Clay to pull recent LinkedIn posts, podcast appearances, and job changes. Then an LLM reads that context and writes a first line that's genuinely specific. Not "congrats on the new role" specific. More like "your point about attribution modeling in that Pavilion talk tracks with something we're seeing with 50-person revops teams" specific.

Reply rates went from 3% to 11%. Not because the tech is magic. Because the inputs are better.

The boring truth: personalization quality is an enrichment problem, not a copywriting problem. Fix your inputs and mediocre templates outperform clever ones with bad data.
posts.cta: What enrichment sources are you actually using for personalization beyond the basics?

inspired_by.author: Shruti Govil
inspired_by.summary: Explains how to structure revenue operations for high-growth SaaS, blending sales ops with GTM engineering. Shares case study on content strategy that drove 25% pipeline increase via LinkedIn and email sequences. Stresses cross-functional alignment for outsized results.

remixes.label: teardown-examples
remixes.hook: I reviewed 200 outbound emails last month for a client. 194 of them were "personalized" with the exact same pattern. Here's what the other 6 did differently.
remixes.body:
I reviewed 200 outbound emails last month for a client. 194 of them were "personalized" with the exact same pattern. Here's what the other 6 did differently.

The 194 all followed this template: "Hey {name}, I noticed {company} just {trigger event from a data vendor}. We help companies like yours {value prop}." The recipient sees through this in two seconds. Not because the data is wrong. Because the structure is identical to every other cold email in their inbox.

The 6 that got replies did something different. They referenced something specific that required actual research. One mentioned a technical decision visible in the company's public GitHub. Another referenced a comment the prospect left on a LinkedIn post three weeks ago. A third cited a specific pain point from the prospect's own podcast appearance.

The difference isn't data quality. It's research depth. The 194 used data available to every sales tool on the market. The 6 used information that required human-level context to find and connect.

Here's the thing: agents can actually do this now. You can point an agent at a prospect's public footprint, LinkedIn activity, GitHub contributions, podcast appearances, conference talks, and generate genuinely personalized context. Not merge fields. Actual insights about what this specific person cares about.

The bottleneck was never technology. It was the effort required per prospect. Agents collapsed that effort to near zero.
remixes.cta: What's the most creative source of personalization data you've seen in a cold email? Not the obvious ones.

remixes.label: buyer-perspective
remixes.hook: I get 40+ cold emails a day. I've replied to 3 in the last year. All 3 had one thing in common.
remixes.body:
I get 40+ cold emails a day. I've replied to 3 in the last year. All 3 had one thing in common.

They couldn't have been sent to anyone else.

One referenced a specific blog post I wrote and disagreed with a point I made. Not a generic "loved your content" opener. An actual opinion about a specific claim. That takes either real research or genuine interest. Either way, I'm reading the rest of the email.

Another mentioned a technical choice visible in my public repos and suggested an alternative I hadn't considered. They clearly looked at my actual work, not my LinkedIn headline.

The third referenced a comment I left on someone else's post and connected it to a problem their product solves. That's at least 15 minutes of research for a single email. Or 30 seconds with an agent that knows where to look.

Here's what all the other emails have in common: I can tell they were sent to 500 people with variable swaps. The "personalization" is surface-level data anyone can buy. Company name. Recent funding round. Job title.

That's not personalization. That's a mail merge with better data sources.

Real personalization means understanding what I specifically care about, based on my actual public behavior, not my firmographic profile. The tools exist to do this now. Most teams are just using them wrong.
remixes.cta: When's the last time a cold email made you think, "okay, this person actually did their homework"? What made it stand out?

tones.builder:
"Personalization at scale" is the most abused phrase in sales ops right now. Most of what people call personalized is just merge fields with extra steps.

"Hey {first_name}, I saw {company} just {trigger_event}..." is not personalization. It's a template with variables. The recipient knows it. Their spam filter knows it.

Real personalization means your email couldn't have been sent to anyone else. It references something specific they said, built, or published. It connects their problem to your solution in a way that requires actually understanding both.

That's hard to automate. But not impossible.

The pipeline I've been running uses Clay to pull recent LinkedIn posts, podcast appearances, and job changes. Then an LLM reads that context and writes a first line that's genuinely specific. Not "congrats on the new role" specific. More like "your point about attribution modeling in that Pavilion talk tracks with something we're seeing with 50-person revops teams" specific.

Reply rates went from 3% to 11%. Not because the tech is magic. Because the inputs are better.

The boring truth: personalization quality is an enrichment problem, not a copywriting problem. Fix your inputs and mediocre templates outperform clever ones with bad data.
tones.advisor:
If you're running outbound at scale, I'd recommend auditing your personalization strategy against a simple test: could this email have been sent to 100 other people with just a name swap? If yes, it's not personalized. It's templated.

The framework I recommend has three tiers. Tier one is firmographic, company name, industry, size, recent funding. Every tool provides this. It's table stakes, not differentiation. Tier two is behavioral, what the prospect has said, written, or engaged with publicly. LinkedIn comments, blog posts, conference talks, GitHub activity. Tier three is contextual, connecting their specific situation to your specific solution in a way that demonstrates genuine understanding.

Most teams stop at tier one and call it personalization. The teams getting reply rates above 5% are consistently operating at tier two or three.

The good news: agents can now handle tier two research at scale. Point an agent at a prospect's public footprint and it can surface specific talking points, opinions, and interests in seconds. The output quality depends entirely on your prompt design and the sources you point it at.

My recommendation: build enrichment pipelines that go beyond data vendor APIs. Scrape public content. Index it. Let agents synthesize it into genuinely specific outreach. The infrastructure cost is minimal compared to the reply rate improvement.

Your "personalized" outbound is spam with better typography. The recipient knows it. Their spam filter knows it. You're the only one who doesn't know it.

"Hey {first_name}, I noticed {company} just {trigger_event}" is not personalization. It's a template with variables purchased from a data vendor that sold the same variables to your three closest competitors. The prospect got your email, your competitor's email, and two others with the same "personalized" trigger event. All on the same Tuesday.

The outbound industry built an elaborate infrastructure for sending slightly better spam at slightly higher volume. And then everyone acts surprised when reply rates crater year over year.

Want actual personalization? Reference something the prospect said in their own words. A LinkedIn comment. A podcast take. A technical decision visible in their public code. Something that proves you spent more than zero seconds thinking about them specifically.

"But that doesn't scale." It does now. Agents can research a prospect's public footprint in seconds. The technology exists. The problem is that most sales teams would rather send 1,000 templated emails than 100 genuinely personalized ones, because volume metrics look better in the weekly report.

Your reply rate is your actual metric. Everything else is vanity.

posts.title: Solo builder economics in 2026
posts.hook: The math on solo building has completely changed. Not because of AI hype. Because the cost of infrastructure hit the floor.
posts.body:
The math on solo building has completely changed. Not because of AI hype. Because the cost of infrastructure hit the floor.

I run 3 production websites, an autonomous blog generator, a CRM integration pipeline, daily content distribution across 4 platforms, and a client-facing dashboard system. All from a single Mac Mini.

Monthly infrastructure cost: under $200. That covers Vercel, a few API keys, and domain renewals.

Two years ago this would have required a small team. Or at minimum, significant DevOps overhead. Today it's cron jobs, serverless functions, and agents that handle the repetitive work.

The Pieter Levels model works, but the version I'm living is slightly different. It's not just "ship fast and see what sticks." It's "build systems that compound." Every pipeline I build for a client teaches me something I bring to the next one. Every automation I build for myself becomes a template I can deploy for others.

The real unlock isn't that one person can build more. It's that one person can maintain more. The operational ceiling went up by 10x. You can run production systems that serve real clients without a team, without investors, without permission.

That changes who gets to play the game.
posts.cta: Solo builders... what's your actual monthly infrastructure cost? Curious how lean others are running.

remixes.label: cost-breakdown
remixes.hook: Here's my actual monthly bill for running 3 websites, an autonomous blog, a CRM pipeline, and daily content distribution. It's probably less than your team's Slack bill.
remixes.body:
Here's my actual monthly bill for running 3 websites, an autonomous blog, a CRM pipeline, and daily content distribution. It's probably less than your team's Slack bill.

Vercel hosting for 3 production sites: $20/month on the Pro plan. API costs for Claude (content generation, CRM enrichment, daily blog posts): roughly $80/month. Domain registrations: $36/year across three domains, so call it $3/month. Deployment platform for client dashboards: $5/month. Various SaaS tools (email, analytics, monitoring): $40/month. Total: under $150.

The hardware is a single Mac Mini that runs 24/7. It handles all my cron jobs, local development, and acts as the always-on server for automated tasks. I bought it for $1,400 two years ago. Amortized, that's about $60/month.

So the full loaded cost of my entire operation is roughly $200/month. This would have required a small team and five figures in monthly infrastructure costs three years ago.

What changed: serverless compute got cheap enough to be free at my scale. AI APIs replaced entire categories of manual work. And the Mac Mini turned out to be a perfectly capable server for everything that doesn't need to be in the cloud.

I'm not saying everyone should go solo. I'm saying the economics of going solo have never been this favorable. The minimum viable team for a real software business is now one person with the right stack.
remixes.cta: What's your all-in monthly cost to run your projects? I bet more people are running leaner than they think.

remixes.label: permission-to-start
remixes.hook: You don't need a cofounder, funding, or a team to build a real business in 2026. You need a Mac Mini and about $200/month.
remixes.body:
You don't need a cofounder, funding, or a team to build a real business in 2026. You need a Mac Mini and about $200/month.

I keep meeting people who want to build but feel like they need permission. They think they need a technical cofounder. Or seed funding for infrastructure. Or at least enough money for a small engineering team.

The infrastructure argument died sometime in the last 18 months. Here's what actually happened.

Serverless platforms like Vercel made hosting effectively free at small scale. AI APIs replaced the need for specialized hires in content, data enrichment, and basic ops work. A single Mac Mini can run cron jobs, dev environments, and local AI models simultaneously. Python and TypeScript cover 95% of what a solo builder needs.

I run production websites, automated content pipelines, CRM integrations, and client dashboards. All solo. All from one machine. The total monthly cost would be a rounding error on most companies' AWS bills.

The real barrier was never infrastructure cost. It was the belief that you needed infrastructure cost. The mental model of "real businesses need real infrastructure" made people wait for funding that they never actually needed.

If you have a laptop, an internet connection, and $200/month in budget, you have everything required to build and ship a real product. The only remaining variable is whether you'll actually do it.

The permission you're waiting for isn't coming. Start anyway.
remixes.cta: What's actually stopping you from starting? Not the excuse. The real thing.

The economics of solo building have shifted dramatically, and I'd recommend anyone considering it to actually run the numbers before assuming they need outside capital.

Here's the framework I use. Break your infrastructure into four categories: compute (hosting, serverless functions), AI services (APIs for content, enrichment, automation), tooling (SaaS subscriptions, domain costs), and hardware (amortized cost of your development machine).

For most solo builders, the total across all four categories should be under $300/month. If it's significantly higher, you're likely over-provisioned or paying for tools you're not fully utilizing.

My specific breakdown: $20/month for Vercel Pro hosting three production sites. $80/month in AI API costs for content generation and CRM enrichment. $40/month across various SaaS tools. Hardware amortized at $60/month for a Mac Mini that doubles as an always-on server.

The key insight: don't rent compute you don't need. Serverless platforms charge per request at small scale, which means your hosting bill grows with your revenue, not ahead of it. Use local hardware for cron jobs and development. Reserve cloud spend for customer-facing services.

My recommendation for anyone starting: build on free tiers first. Graduate to paid tiers only when you hit actual limits. Most solo projects never need to leave the free tier for their first year. The constraint isn't budget. It's shipping.

The startup ecosystem convinced an entire generation that building a software business requires $500K in seed funding and a team of eight. That was always a lie. Now it's an obvious lie.

I run three production websites, an autonomous content engine, a full CRM pipeline, and daily multi-platform distribution. Monthly cost: under $200. Hardware: a Mac Mini sitting on my desk.

No DevOps team. No cloud architect. No $50K/month AWS bill that a "fractional CTO" manages while explaining that it's actually very reasonable for this stage.

The venture-backed startup model was never the only way to build. It was just the most visible way because VCs have the best marketing in the industry. They convinced builders that spending $10K/month on infrastructure before having a single customer is "moving fast."

Moving fast is shipping on a $200/month budget so you have infinite runway to figure out product-market fit without burning someone else's money.

The tools caught up. Serverless is effectively free at small scale. AI APIs do the work of junior hires. A $1,400 Mac Mini runs 24/7 as your personal server.

Every week you spend fundraising is a week you could spend building. The math isn't even close anymore.

posts.title: Agent orchestration is the new IC skill
posts.hook: Hot take: "prompt engineering" was the 2024 skill. Agent orchestration is the 2026 skill. And they're nothing alike.
posts.body:
Hot take: "prompt engineering" was the 2024 skill. Agent orchestration is the 2026 skill. And they're nothing alike.

Prompt engineering is about talking to one model well. Agent orchestration is about designing systems where multiple agents coordinate, share context, and produce reliable output without you babysitting them.

I run agent teams for content production. One agent scrapes trends. Another drafts posts. A third runs voice compliance checks. A fourth handles distribution. They share a context protocol so each knows what the others did.

When it works, I wake up to 4 platform-ready drafts waiting for review. When it breaks, I wake up to an agent that confidently posted the same content twice because the memory handoff failed.

The skill isn't writing better prompts. It's designing failure modes. Knowing where to add checkpoints. Understanding which tasks can run in parallel and which need sequential gates. Deciding when an agent should ask for help vs. retry vs. fail loudly.

This is systems thinking applied to AI. And the people who are best at it aren't the ML researchers. They're the ops people. The ones who already think in pipelines, error handling, and state management.

Eliot Peper's prediction about developer roles shifting toward orchestration is already happening. The shift just looks more like DevOps than data science.
posts.cta: What's broken in your agent workflows right now? The failure modes are where all the learning lives.

inspired_by.author: Eliot Peper
inspired_by.summary: Analyzes the economic impact of AI-driven automation on developer roles, predicting a shift towards agent orchestration expertise. Reviews top AI infrastructure providers like Pinecone and LangChain for agent persistence. Warns of bottlenecks in current tooling stacks.

remixes.label: systems-thinking-angle
remixes.hook: The engineers who will dominate the next 3 years aren't the ones writing the best code. They're the ones designing the best agent systems.
remixes.body:
The engineers who will dominate the next 3 years aren't the ones writing the best code. They're the ones designing the best agent systems.

I used to spend my mornings manually reviewing trend data, drafting content, and scheduling posts. Now I have a team of agents that handles 80% of that pipeline before I open my laptop.

But about agent orchestration. It's not about prompts. It's about failure handling, context sharing, and knowing when to let an agent retry vs. when to escalate to a human.

Three things I learned building my agent team:

First, agents need clear boundaries. One agent, one job. The moment you give an agent two responsibilities, its output quality drops.

Second, shared context is harder than it sounds. You need a system for passing state between agents that doesn't break when one agent hallucinates or returns garbage.

Third, monitoring matters more than prompting. I spend more time reviewing agent outputs and tuning handoff logic than I do writing prompts.

This isn't prompt engineering with extra steps. It's systems design. If you've ever built microservices, the mental model transfers directly. Loose coupling, clear contracts, observability.
remixes.cta: What's the first agent workflow you'd automate if reliability wasn't a concern?

remixes.label: hiring-lens
remixes.hook: I'd rather hire someone who can orchestrate 5 AI agents than someone who can write perfect prompts all day.
remixes.body:
I'd rather hire someone who can orchestrate 5 AI agents than someone who can write perfect prompts all day.

Prompt engineering was a useful skill. Past tense. It got people comfortable talking to models. But talking to one model is table stakes now.

The hard part in 2026 is building systems where agents work together. Where one agent's output feeds into another's input. Where failures get caught and rerouted instead of silently corrupting your pipeline.

I run agent teams daily. Content scraping, enrichment, drafting, publishing. The prompts inside each agent are simple. The orchestration layer is where all the complexity lives.

Think about it like managing a team of junior developers. Each one can do their job fine in isolation. The challenge is coordination. Who goes first? What happens when someone's output is wrong? How do you avoid bottlenecks?

The people who figure this out will build 10x more than those still optimizing individual prompts. Because the leverage isn't in one perfect conversation with a model. It's in designing reliable systems that run without you.

This is an IC skill, not a management skill. You're not delegating. You're architecting.
remixes.cta: If you're building agent workflows, what's the biggest coordination problem you've hit?

Here's : the gap between prompt engineers and agent orchestrators is going to look like the gap between people who could use Excel and people who could build software. Both useful. One dramatically more valuable.

Stop optimizing your system prompts. Start building systems.

posts.title: Revenue operations without the bloat
posts.hook: Unpopular opinion: most revops teams are over-tooled and under-wired. 12 tools in the stack, none of them talking to each other properly.
posts.body:
Unpopular opinion: most revops teams are over-tooled and under-wired. 12 tools in the stack, none of them talking to each other properly.

I audited a client's GTM stack last month. They had Apollo, ZoomInfo, Outreach, Salesforce, Clari, Gong, and 4 other tools I'd never heard of. Annual spend north of $180k.

Their enrichment data was stale. Their sequences weren't personalized. Their pipeline reporting was a mess because data lived in 3 different systems with conflicting definitions of "qualified."

We ripped out 7 tools. Replaced them with Clay for enrichment, Instantly for delivery, Attio for CRM, and a few Python scripts for the glue. Total cost dropped to under $30k/year.

Pipeline actually went up. Not because the tools were better. Because when your stack is simple enough to understand, you can actually diagnose what's working and fix what isn't.

The revops industry has a tool addiction problem. Every pain point gets a new SaaS subscription instead of a better workflow. But the teams generating the most pipeline per dollar are running lean stacks with tight integrations.

Three tools wired well beats twelve tools wired poorly. Every time.
posts.cta: How many tools are in your GTM stack right now? Genuinely curious, no judgment.

inspired_by.author: Josh Braun
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down outbound automation myths and shares a humanized approach combining sales ops tools with genuine prospecting. Discusses content strategy for thought leadership that complements automated outreach. Backed by data from 100+ sales teams.

remixes.label: audit-teardown
remixes.hook: I helped a company cut $140k from their GTM stack last month. They didn't lose a single capability.
remixes.body:
I helped a company cut $140k from their GTM stack last month. They didn't lose a single capability.

Here's what I found when I mapped their data flow: most of their tools were doing overlapping jobs. Three different enrichment sources feeding stale data into a CRM nobody trusted. Two outreach tools running sequences that weren't personalized. A forecasting tool that just re-displayed Salesforce reports.

The fix wasn't adding better tools. It was wiring fewer tools together properly.

We consolidated enrichment to one source with real-time validation. Replaced their outreach stack with a single tool connected to live CRM data. Killed the forecasting tool entirely because their CRM could do the same thing natively.

Stack went from 12 tools to 5. Monthly cost dropped by 60%. But here's the real win: their data actually flows now. Enrichment feeds directly into sequences. Engagement signals update the CRM in real time. Pipeline visibility went from "check three dashboards" to "look at one screen."

The problem with revops isn't the tools. It's that every tool gets purchased to solve a point problem without anyone asking how it connects to everything else. You end up with a pile of solutions and no system.
remixes.cta: When's the last time you actually mapped the data flow across your GTM stack?

remixes.label: builder-alternative
remixes.hook: I replaced a $180k/year GTM stack with 4 scripts and a CRM. Not because I'm cheap. Because the scripts actually work.
remixes.body:
I replaced a $180k/year GTM stack with 4 scripts and a CRM. Not because I'm cheap. Because the scripts actually work.

Most revops stacks are Frankenstein monsters. Each tool was bought to solve a real problem. But nobody designed the system. So you end up with 12 tools, 4 sources of truth, and a team that spends more time reconciling data than acting on it.

What I built instead: a Python enrichment script that pulls from multiple APIs and deduplicates in real time. An automated sequence builder that personalizes based on actual firmographic data, not templates. A scoring model that updates nightly. And a CRM that's the single source of truth for everything.

Total cost: about $2k/month in API fees plus the CRM license.

The key insight isn't that tools are bad. It's that integration is the product. A $50k enrichment tool that feeds stale data into sequences that aren't connected to your CRM is worth less than a script that keeps everything in sync.

I know this approach isn't for everyone. Not every team has someone who can build and maintain custom pipelines. But if you do, the ROI is absurd. Not just cost savings. Better data, faster execution, fewer places where things silently break.
remixes.cta: Has anyone else gone the build-your-own route for revops? What broke first?

tones.builder:
Unpopular opinion: most revops teams are over-tooled and under-wired. 12 tools in the stack, none of them talking to each other properly.

I audited a client's GTM stack last month. They had Apollo, ZoomInfo, Outreach, Salesforce, Clari, Gong, and 4 other tools I'd never heard of. Annual spend north of $180k.

Their enrichment data was stale. Their sequences weren't personalized. Their pipeline reporting was a mess because data lived in 3 different systems with conflicting definitions of "qualified."

We ripped out 7 tools. Replaced them with Clay for enrichment, Instantly for delivery, Attio for CRM, and a few Python scripts for the glue. Total cost dropped to under $30k/year.

Pipeline actually went up. Not because the tools were better. Because when your stack is simple enough to understand, you can actually diagnose what's working and fix what isn't.

The revops industry has a tool addiction problem. Every pain point gets a new SaaS subscription instead of a better workflow. But the teams generating the most pipeline per dollar are running lean stacks with tight integrations.

Three tools wired well beats twelve tools wired poorly. Every time.

My recommendation for any revops leader right now: before you buy the next tool, map your actual data flow. Where does a lead enter? What enrichment happens? How does it reach a rep? Where does engagement data go back?

Most teams will find that 40 to 60 percent of their stack is redundant or underutilized. The solution isn't more tools. It's better wiring between fewer tools. Consolidate enrichment sources. Ensure CRM is the single source of truth. Make sure engagement signals flow back in real time, not in batch uploads someone runs on Fridays.

posts.title: Career pivots look obvious in hindsight
posts.hook: Nobody talks about the 6 months between "I'm leaving" and "this is working." That middle part is where most people quit.
posts.body:
Nobody talks about the 6 months between "I'm leaving" and "this is working." That middle part is where most people quit.

When I see people like Sahil or Daniel Vassallo share their pivot stories, the narrative is clean. Left big company. Built thing. Thing worked. But zoom into the timeline and there's always a gap where nothing is working, revenue is zero, and you're questioning every decision.

I went through that. Left a sales role, started building GTM systems as a solo operator. First 3 months were brutal. No clients, no portfolio, no proof that any of this would work.

What got me through wasn't a strategy. It was building in public. Shipping something every day, even when nobody was watching. A Clay workflow. An enrichment script. A blog post about what I learned that week.

Eventually the work compounds. Someone sees a post, needs exactly that thing, and now you have a client. That client has a friend. The friend has a company that needs the same pipeline.

But none of that happens if you stop shipping during the gap. The gap is the filter. It separates people who pivot from people who just fantasize about it.

If you're in the gap right now, the only advice I have: ship something today. Anything. The streak matters more than the output.
posts.cta: For those who've made a career pivot... what kept you going during the gap?

inspired_by.author: Sahil Lavingia
inspired_by.summary: Sahil shared insights on his career pivot from Pinterest to founding Gumroad as a solo builder initially, stressing minimal viable products and public iteration. He advised aspiring founders on shipping fast without teams. The thread went viral among indie makers.

remixes.label: survival-tactics
remixes.hook: The pivot story everyone shares: "I left, I built, it worked." The real story: 4 months of nothing working, savings draining, and wondering if you made the worst decision of your life.
remixes.body:
The pivot story everyone shares: "I left, I built, it worked." The real story: 4 months of nothing working, savings draining, and wondering if you made the worst decision of your life.

I've been through it. The gap between "I'm doing this" and "this is actually working" is the loneliest stretch of any career. Your former colleagues are posting promotions. Your family is quietly worried. Your bank account has an opinion.

What got me through wasn't motivation or vision. It was structure. I gave myself rules:

Ship something every week, even if it's small. Don't let a week pass where you can't point to something concrete you built or launched.

Talk to real people, not just your Twitter timeline. Isolation kills more pivots than bad ideas do.

Set a financial tripwire. Mine was: if runway hits 3 months, I take a contract to extend it. No shame in that. It's not quitting, it's buying time.

Track inputs, not outcomes. Early on, revenue is a lagging indicator. Track the things you can control. Conversations had. Things shipped. Outreach sent.

The gap is real. It's supposed to be hard. But most people who quit the gap quit because they didn't have a system for surviving it, not because their idea was wrong.
remixes.cta: What's one rule or system that kept you sane during a major career transition?

remixes.label: myth-busting
remixes.hook: Career pivots don't fail because of bad timing or bad ideas. They fail because of bad narratives. The story you tell yourself during the gap determines everything.
remixes.body:
Career pivots don't fail because of bad timing or bad ideas. They fail because of bad narratives. The story you tell yourself during the gap determines everything.

I've watched people with great skills and solid ideas give up at month 3 because they convinced themselves that discomfort meant failure. Meanwhile, others with half the talent pushed through because they expected the gap and planned for it.

The dangerous narrative: "If this was the right move, it would feel right by now." That's not how pivots work. The first 6 months feel wrong almost by definition. You're building new skills, new reputation, new revenue streams from zero. Of course it doesn't feel right. You're a beginner again.

The useful narrative: "This is the tax I pay for building something I actually own." Every day in the gap is an investment. The skills you're building, the network you're forming, the product you're shipping. None of that disappears if the first version doesn't work.

When I see founders like Sahil Lavingia or Daniel Vassallo share their stories, the timeline looks clean in retrospect. But they all had a gap. They all had months where nothing was working. The difference is they expected it.

Expect the gap. Budget for the gap. Build a system for surviving the gap. The idea can be refined later. The gap is what kills most pivots before the idea even gets a fair shot.
remixes.cta: What's the worst narrative you told yourself during a big transition? Looking back, was it true?

posts.title: AI SDK production patterns
posts.hook: I've shipped 4 AI features to production this year. Here's the pattern that survived every one of them.
posts.body:
I've shipped 4 AI features to production this year. Here's the pattern that survived every one of them.

Start with the dumbest possible version. No streaming. No fancy UI. Just a form that sends a prompt and returns text. Get that working end to end in production before adding complexity.

Then layer in one thing at a time:

1. Streaming response so users aren't staring at a spinner for 8 seconds
2. Error handling that actually tells the user what went wrong
3. Rate limiting before someone finds your endpoint and burns your API credits
4. Memory/context if the feature needs conversation history

Every time I've tried to build all four at once, something breaks in a way that's impossible to debug. Is the streaming broken or the memory? Is the error from rate limiting or the model? You can't tell.

The Vercel AI SDK handles most of this well if you let it. But I see teams wrapping it in so many abstractions that they lose the ability to debug. One team had 6 layers between the user input and the model call. Six.

Keep the path from user input to model output as short as possible. Add indirection only when you have a specific reason, not because it feels "clean."

Production AI features are boring plumbing problems. Treat them that way.
posts.cta: What's bitten you hardest shipping AI features to production?

inspired_by.author: Guillermo Rauch
inspired_by.summary: Announces updates to Vercel's AI SDK for building production-grade AI agents with seamless integration into developer workflows. Discusses challenges in AI infrastructure scaling for edge deployment. Provides code examples for automating Next.js apps with agents.

remixes.label: failure-first
remixes.hook: Every AI feature I've shipped to production broke in the same way the first time. Here's the failure pattern nobody warns you about.
remixes.body:
Every AI feature I've shipped to production broke in the same way the first time. Here's the failure pattern nobody warns you about.

You build the demo. It works great locally. You ship it. Users hit it with real inputs. Everything falls apart. Not because the model is bad, but because you didn't account for the gap between demo conditions and production reality.

The inputs are longer, weirder, and more adversarial than anything in your test set. Latency spikes because you're streaming without proper timeout handling. Error states show raw model output to users. The fallback when the API is down is a blank screen.

After breaking things the same way four times, I developed a checklist:

Start with the dumbest possible version. No streaming. No fancy UI. A form that sends a prompt and returns text. Get that working end to end in production before touching anything else.

Add streaming second. But build the timeout and fallback states first, before the happy path.

Structured output before free text. Use schemas to constrain the model's output so your frontend can actually render it reliably.

Log everything. Every prompt, every response, every latency measurement. You can't debug production AI without traces.

Rate limit from day one. Not just for cost. For user experience. A queue is better than a timeout error.
remixes.cta: What's the most surprising production failure you've had with an AI feature?

remixes.label: progressive-complexity
remixes.hook: Stop building AI features top-down. The teams shipping reliably all build bottom-up. Here's the difference.
remixes.body:
Stop building AI features top-down. The teams shipping reliably all build bottom-up. Here's the difference.

Top-down: you design the ideal UX, pick the fanciest model, add streaming and tool calling and multi-step reasoning, then try to make it all work. It never works the first time. You spend weeks debugging a system with too many moving parts.

Bottom-up: you start with the simplest possible version. A text input. A single API call. A text output. No streaming. No tools. You ship that to production. Then you layer complexity one piece at a time, validating each layer before adding the next.

I've shipped 4 AI features this year. Every single one followed the same progression:

Layer 0: form input, prompt, text response. Ship it.
Layer 1: streaming response. Validate that reconnection and error states work.
Layer 2: structured output. Constrain the model so the frontend can render predictably.
Layer 3: context and memory. Add conversation history or RAG, but only after the base works.
Layer 4: tools and actions. Let the model do things, but with guardrails and human confirmation.

Each layer gets its own deploy and its own validation. If layer 2 breaks, you roll back to layer 1 while you fix it. Users always have a working product.

The teams that struggle are the ones trying to ship layer 4 on day one.

posts.title: Content strategy for builders
posts.hook: The best content strategy for a builder is not a content strategy. It's a build log with distribution.
posts.body:
The best content strategy for a builder is not a content strategy. It's a build log with distribution.

I post what I build. That's it. No editorial calendar. No content pillars deck. No brainstorming sessions about "what resonates."

Monday I built an enrichment pipeline. Wrote about it Tuesday. Wednesday I automated my deploy workflow with an agent. Posted about it Thursday. Friday I debugged a webhook race condition. That became Saturday's post.

The content writes itself because the work is the content. And it performs because it's specific. Real tool names. Real numbers. Real problems with real solutions.

Compare that to the "5 tips for better outbound" posts that flood LinkedIn. No specifics. No proof. No reason to believe the person writing it has ever actually sent a cold email.

Builders have an unfair advantage in content: they have unlimited source material. Every day produces something worth sharing. A workflow that worked. A tool that didn't. A number that surprised you.

The distribution part is simpler than people make it. Pick 2 platforms. Post consistently. Put the real value in the comments (prompts, templates, configs). Let the work speak.

You don't need a content strategy. You need to build interesting things and tell people about them.
posts.cta: Builders who post regularly... do you plan content or just document what you're building?

inspired_by.author: Aakash Gupta
inspired_by.summary: Details engineering GTM motions with heavy outbound automation and revops playbooks. Covers content syndication strategies that fed 50% of pipeline. Practical advice for sales ops to operationalize at scale.

remixes.label: anti-strategy
remixes.hook: I deleted my content calendar 6 months ago. My engagement went up. My stress went down. Here's what I do instead.
remixes.body:
I deleted my content calendar 6 months ago. My engagement went up. My stress went down. Here's what I do instead.

I build things. Then I write about what I built. That's the entire strategy.

Monday I built an enrichment pipeline. Wrote about it Tuesday. Wednesday I automated my deploy workflow with an agent. Posted about it Thursday. Friday I debugged a nasty race condition. That became a thread.

No brainstorming sessions. No "what resonates" meetings. No pillar frameworks. Just work, documented.

Here's why it works better than planned content: authenticity is hard to fake and easy to detect. When you write about something you actually did yesterday, the specificity comes through. You mention the exact error message. The specific tool that broke. The workaround that saved you two hours.

Planned content tends toward the generic because you're writing about abstract ideas instead of concrete experiences. "5 tips for better automation" vs. "I automated my deploy and here's the one thing that almost broke it." The second one always wins.

The other advantage: zero writer's block. You never have to come up with ideas because you're not generating ideas. You're just reporting on work you already did. The content is a byproduct of the building, not a separate activity.

If you're building things and not posting about them, you're leaving the easiest content on the table.
remixes.cta: What did you build this week that could be a post?

remixes.label: distribution-mechanics
remixes.hook: "Just build in public" is incomplete advice. Building is the easy part. Distribution is where most builders get stuck.
remixes.body:
"Just build in public" is incomplete advice. Building is the easy part. Distribution is where most builders get stuck.

I post what I build. That's the content strategy. But the part nobody talks about is how you turn a build log into something people actually see.

Three things I learned:

First, the build log needs a hook. "I built an enrichment pipeline" is not a post. "I replaced a $180k tool stack with 4 Python scripts" is a post. Same work, different framing. The framing is what earns the click.

Second, ship the insight, not the tutorial. Nobody on LinkedIn wants a step-by-step walkthrough. They want the one surprising thing you learned. The decision that felt counterintuitive. The result that challenged an assumption.

Third, cross-post with platform awareness. What works on LinkedIn doesn't work on X. LinkedIn wants the narrative arc with a question at the end. X wants the sharpest version of the insight in 280 characters. Same source material, different packaging.

I write once from the build log, then adapt for each platform. Total time: about 30 minutes per build session. The ROI compounds because every post is backed by real work, which means real specifics, which means real engagement.

Content strategy for builders isn't about content. It's about making the work you're already doing visible.
remixes.cta: What's the best post you've written that came directly from something you built?

The overhead is minimal because you're not generating ideas. You're reporting on work you already did. And the engagement tends to be higher because specific, experience-based content consistently outperforms generic advice.
tones.provocateur:
Content calendars are where builder authenticity goes to die.

You know what happens when a builder sits down for a "content brainstorming session"? They stop thinking about what they built and start thinking about what will perform. And performance-optimized content from builders is indistinguishable from content from people who don't build anything.

My content strategy: I build things. I write about what I built. No calendar. No pillars. No brainstorming.

Monday I built an enrichment pipeline. Wrote about it Tuesday. Wednesday I automated deploys with an agent. Posted Thursday. Friday I debugged a race condition. That became a thread.

Zero writer's block. Zero time spent "ideating." And my engagement went up because people can smell the difference between real build logs and manufactured thought leadership.

The content industry has convinced builders that they need a strategy. You don't. You need to ship things and then tell people what happened. The specificity of real work is your unfair advantage over every content creator who's never pushed code to production.

If you're maintaining a content calendar and building things, you're doing twice the work for half the authenticity. Pick one. I picked building.

platforms.linkedin:

The content writes itself because the work is the content. And it performs because it's specific. Real tool names. Real numbers. Real problems with real solutions.

Compare that to the "5 tips for better outbound" posts that flood LinkedIn. No specifics. No proof. No reason to believe the person writing it has ever actually sent a cold email.

Builders have an unfair advantage in content: they have unlimited source material. Every day produces something worth sharing. A workflow that worked. A tool that didn't. A number that surprised you.

The distribution part is simpler than people make it. Pick 2 platforms. Post consistently. Put the real value in the comments (prompts, templates, configs). Let the work speak.

You don't need a content strategy. You need to build interesting things and tell people about them.
